responses.explanation: Don't worry about lifting heavy on day one — focus on movement quality. Reps in good form beat heavy reps in bad form, every time.

Looking to progressively overload from {recentWorkout}?

patterns: should i lift heavy or light
is full body or split better
how long should a workout be
what's the best way to train

responses.shortAnswer: **Pick 3–4 sessions a week** and stick to compound lifts.
responses.explanation: Compound lifts (squat, deadlift, press, row) hit lots of muscle per minute. As a beginner, just adding weight or one rep each week is enough — that's progressive overload.
actionSteps: Add a small bump (weight or rep) every week
responses.suggestion: Want me to open your current plan?

responses.shortAnswer: Cycle intensity: **3–6 reps for strength, 8–12 for size, 12–20 for endurance**.
responses.explanation: Periodise volume across 4–6 week blocks. Push the main lift to RPE 8–9, keep accessories at RPE 7. Track e1RM weekly.
responses.suggestion: Want a deload primer?

responses.explanation: {firstName}, you've already done {totalWorkouts} sessions. Most people quit before this point — you didn't. Keep showing up and the results compound.
responses.suggestion: Need help making the next session easier to start?

responses.shortAnswer: Here's a clean weekly template you can start with.
responses.suggestion: Want me to regenerate your plan to match?

responses.shortAnswer: **Volume × intensity × frequency** — pick two to push, third stays moderate.
responses.explanation: 10–20 working sets/muscle/week is the sweet spot for hypertrophy. Frequency 2x/week per muscle beats 1x. Form quality non-negotiable past RPE 8.
actionSteps: Hit each muscle 2x with 5–10 sets each
Use AMRAP top sets to gauge progress

responses.shortAnswer: Train, eat, sleep — all three or none of them.
responses.explanation: Training is the spark. Food is the fuel. Sleep is when you actually grow. Aim for 7–9h sleep on training days.
responses.suggestion: Want nutrition or recovery tips next?

patterns: how much protein do i need
how many calories should i eat
what's a good post workout meal
what should i eat for breakfast
what should my diet look like
what to eat to build muscle
what to eat to lose weight

responses.shortAnswer: **Protein with every meal**, eat real food, drink water.
responses.explanation: For your goal ({goal}), aim for 1.6–2.2 g of protein per kg of bodyweight per day. Build meals around a protein, a carb, veggies, and a fat.
actionSteps: Pick a protein: chicken, fish, eggs, tofu, beans
Add a carb: rice, oats, potato, bread
Add veggies and a fat: oil, nuts, avocado
responses.suggestion: Want a quick macro target?

responses.explanation: Goal {goal}: fat loss = ~300–500 kcal deficit, muscle gain = ~200–300 kcal surplus, maintenance = match TDEE. Carbs scale with training volume.

actionSteps: **Pre (1–2h before)**: carbs + a little protein (oats + Greek yogurt)
Throughout day: hit your protein target
responses.suggestion: Want supplement guidance too?

responses.shortAnswer: Only **two supplements** are worth it: creatine + whey if needed.
responses.explanation: Creatine monohydrate (3–5 g/day) — most studied legal performance supplement. Whey protein only if you struggle to hit protein from food.
actionSteps: Creatine: 5 g daily, any time, with water
Whey: 1 scoop = ~25 g protein, use to fill gaps
Skip pre-workouts unless you really want one

responses.explanation: Training without nutrition is paddling against the current. Get one thing right this week — protein in every meal — and watch what happens.
responses.suggestion: Want a 1-week beginner meal template?

responses.shortAnswer: **Hydration matters too** — aim for 30–40 ml/kg on training days.
responses.explanation: Most people undereat protein and underdrink water. Fix those two first before worrying about anything fancy.
actionSteps: Pee should be pale yellow, not dark

patterns: i don't feel like working out

responses.shortAnswer: **You don't have to feel like it. You just have to start.**
responses.explanation: Most days the motivation comes after the warm-up, not before. Promise yourself 10 minutes — most of the time the rest follows.
actionSteps: Decide after that — most days you'll keep going
responses.suggestion: Want to open today's session?

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, your **{streak}-day streak** is on the line.
responses.explanation: Even a 15-min easy session keeps the chain alive. Future you remembers what past you did when it was hard.
responses.suggestion: Just press start — the hard part is the first rep.

responses.shortAnswer: Tired and lazy aren't the same thing — figure out which.
responses.explanation: If you slept badly or trained yesterday, take real rest. If it's just a bad mood, the easiest exercise on the list usually fixes it.
actionSteps: Ask: am I sore from yesterday?
If both no → start with one easy lift

responses.shortAnswer: You've banked **{totalWorkouts} workouts**. Don't break the chain.
responses.explanation: Discipline beats motivation. Showing up on the bad days is what separates progress from plateaus.
responses.suggestion: Open the session — 10 minutes is all I'm asking.

actionSteps: **Step 4**: Tap Start — you're past the hardest part

responses.shortAnswer: If it's been 3+ days of fatigue, **rest** — don't push.
responses.explanation: Real burnout needs real rest. A forced session on top of exhaustion sets you back. Take 1–2 days off, then ease in.
responses.suggestion: Want recovery tips for a real rest day?

responses.shortAnswer: Last session was **{lastWorkoutDaysAgo} day(s) ago** — that's why it feels heavy.
responses.explanation: Your adherence is **{adherenceLabel}** right now. The longer the gap the higher the activation cost. The fix is short, easy, and on the calendar — not long, hard, and aspirational.
actionSteps: Pick the easiest item on today's plan
Stop guilt-free if you hit the bell

patterns: should i take a rest day
do i need a rest day
what to do on rest days
how many rest days per week
is it okay to skip a workout

responses.shortAnswer: **Yes — 1 to 2 full rest days/week** is the sweet spot.
responses.explanation: Muscle grows during recovery, not the workout. Hard training without rest = injury or plateau.
actionSteps: Take 1–2 rest days/week (more if very sore)
Easy walk, stretch, or yoga is fine
Skip anything that spikes heart rate
responses.suggestion: Want active recovery ideas?
Need stretching or mobility ideas?

responses.explanation: DOMS up to ~48h is normal. Sharp, sudden, or asymmetric pain isn't — that's a red flag for injury.

responses.shortAnswer: Rest is a tool, not a cheat.
responses.explanation: {firstName}, your {daysPerWeek}-day plan already builds in rest. Trust it. Rest days protect every other day.
responses.suggestion: Want a deload-week plan?

responses.shortAnswer: Every 6–8 weeks, **deload** — drop weights to ~60% for one week.
responses.explanation: A planned light week lets joints, tendons, and your nervous system catch up. You'll come back stronger.
actionSteps: Resume normal loads the next week

patterns: i think i hurt my shoulder

responses.shortAnswer: **Stop loading the area and book a physio or doctor.**
responses.explanation: I'm not qualified to diagnose injuries. Sharp, sudden, or persistent pain needs a real professional eye. Pushing through can turn a 1-week issue into a 3-month one.
actionSteps: Ice if acute and swelling (10–15 min)
Train around it, not through it
responses.suggestion: Want exercises that avoid that area?

responses.explanation: Dull soreness fades in 24–72h. Sharp, swelling, or pain that affects daily life means see a professional ASAP.
actionSteps: Bring that info to a physio

responses.shortAnswer: Train around it. Pick exercises that don't load the painful area.
responses.explanation: If your shoulder hurts on bench, try push-ups on dumbbells or work legs/back instead. Don't lose the habit — just adapt the lifts.
actionSteps: Swap that lift for a pain-free alternative
responses.suggestion: Need exercise swaps for a specific area?

responses.shortAnswer: I can't give medical advice — please see a qualified physio or doctor.
responses.explanation: Your safety matters more than any training plan. Get a real diagnosis, follow their plan, and ease back in only when cleared.
responses.suggestion: Want a guide to finding a sports physio?

responses.shortAnswer: First-aid checklist for a fresh injury.

Pick a version with what you have

**Overhead press** → dumbbell press or pike push-ups

responses.explanation: Slow eccentrics (4–5 sec down), pause reps, single-leg variations all spike difficulty without adding load.

patterns: how many reps for fat loss
how many reps to build muscle

responses.suggestion: Want me to tailor it to your goal?

responses.explanation: 10–20 working sets per muscle group per week is the hypertrophy sweet spot. Spread across 2 sessions/muscle.

responses.shortAnswer: **3 sets of 10** is a great starting default.
responses.explanation: Stop sets ~2 reps before failure (RPE 8). Form > load > volume.
responses.suggestion: Want a starter routine?

actionSteps: Rest 2–3 min on big lifts, 60–90s on others

responses.shortAnswer: Periodise: hypertrophy block → strength block → deload.
responses.explanation: Cycle 4–6 week blocks. Avoid grinding the same scheme forever — adaptation stalls.

patterns: how do i do a row
how to do a hip hinge

actionSteps: Depth: thighs parallel or just below
responses.suggestion: Need form on a different lift?

actionSteps: Hips back, neutral spine, lats engaged
Lock out hips and knees together
Feet planted, slight arch, shoulder blades retracted

responses.explanation: If you can't keep neutral spine or full ROM, the weight is too heavy. Strip it back, fix the pattern, then add weight.

actionSteps: **Squat**: knees track toes, chest tall, drive through midfoot
**Deadlift**: lats tight, push the floor, hips/shoulders rise together
**Bench**: tuck shoulder blades, elbows ~45°, leg drive

actionSteps: **Back squat** → goblet squat, split squat, leg press
**Pull-up** → inverted row, lat pulldown, band-assisted pull-up

responses.explanation: Or open Settings → Edit Profile to update your equipment, and I'll regenerate a plan that already fits.

Match rep range, not necessarily weight

patterns: should i eat carbs at night
how many calories should i eat

actionSteps: Hit protein first, then calories, then split
responses.suggestion: Want a meal template that hits these?

responses.shortAnswer: Calories first, protein second, distribution third.
responses.explanation: TDEE estimate = bodyweight (kg) × 30–35. Adjust ±300 for goal. Lock protein, then carbs scale to training volume.
actionSteps: Carbs 3–6 g/kg, fat fills the rest

responses.shortAnswer: Quick rule: **protein at every meal, carbs around workouts.**
responses.explanation: If counting calories feels overwhelming, just do that. Most people who follow this rule alone end up hitting their macros.
responses.suggestion: Want a sample day of meals?

actionSteps: No carb fear at night — total daily intake matters more

patterns: how much water should i drink

responses.shortAnswer: Aim for **30–40 ml per kg** of bodyweight on training days.
actionSteps: 500 ml in the 2 hours before training
responses.suggestion: Want electrolyte guidance for long sessions?

responses.explanation: Pale straw yellow = good. Dark = drink more. Clear = a bit too much (electrolytes diluted).
actionSteps: Salt + lemon water = cheap homemade option

responses.shortAnswer: Most people are mildly dehydrated and don't know it.
responses.explanation: Headaches, low energy, and bad sleep often trace back to under-drinking. Bottle on the desk + bottle by the bed = solved.
responses.suggestion: Easy daily target?

responses.explanation: Sleep is when growth hormone peaks and muscles actually rebuild. Less than 6h tanks training quality and recovery.
responses.suggestion: Want recovery tactics for poor-sleep days?

responses.explanation: Chronic <6h sleep cuts protein synthesis ~18% and raises cortisol. No program out-trains a sleep deficit.

responses.shortAnswer: Slept badly? **Don't go heavy.** Go easy or take a rest day.
responses.explanation: Forcing a heavy session on 4h sleep is a fast track to injury. Lighter loads, fewer sets, or full rest is the smart play.
actionSteps: Or skip and take a real rest day

responses.shortAnswer: Constantly exhausted = pull back, not push harder.
responses.explanation: {firstName}, if you've been tired multiple days in a row, the answer is rest, not more discipline.
actionSteps: If still tired after a week — see a doctor
**+ Load**: add 2.5–5 kg to the main lift
responses.suggestion: Pick one lever and apply this week.

responses.explanation: Don't change everything at once — pick one variable per week. Overshooting = form breakdown + injury.

responses.shortAnswer: Need it **easier**? That's smart — don't grind hurt.
responses.explanation: Drop weight 10–20%, drop a set, or take a real rest day. You can't progress what you can't recover from.
actionSteps: Or swap for an easier variant (e.g. push-up on knees)

responses.explanation: Hard sessions build the most. But "hard" doesn't mean "all-out." It means uncomfortable, not unsafe.
responses.suggestion: Open today's session?

quiz.question: Which macro is heaviest in 100g of chicken breast?
quiz.explanation: Chicken breast is ~31g protein per 100g — protein-dominant by a wide margin.

quiz.question: What rep range is best for **pure strength**?
quiz.explanation: 3–6 reps with heavier loads is the sweet spot for strength. 8–12 is more for size.

quiz.question: How much protein per kg of bodyweight is recommended for muscle gain?
quiz.explanation: 1.6–2.2 g/kg is the well-researched range. More than 2.2 g/kg has diminishing returns.

quiz.question: Best rest between heavy compound sets?
quiz.explanation: 2–4 min of rest lets you recover enough for the next set without losing strength output.

quiz.question: How many hours of sleep should an active person aim for on training days?
quiz.explanation: 7–9 hours is the recovery sweet spot. <6h tanks training quality and recovery.

quiz.question: Standard daily creatine monohydrate dose?
quiz.explanation: 3–5 g/day is the well-studied maintenance dose. No need to load if you're patient.

quiz.question: Roughly how much water per kg of bodyweight on training days?
quiz.explanation: 30–40 ml/kg covers most active people. More if you sweat heavily.

patterns: how to brace for a squat
low bar vs high bar squat

actionSteps: Bar on upper traps, feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out
Brace your core like you're about to take a punch
Sit hips back AND down, knees track over toes
Hit at least parallel — hip crease below the knee cap
Drive through the whole foot to stand tall
responses.suggestion: Want help picking the load for today?

responses.shortAnswer: Squats hit quads, glutes, adductors, and core.
responses.explanation: High-bar biases quads; low-bar biases posterior chain. Stance width and bar position swap which tissues do the work.
actionSteps: Quads dominant: high-bar, narrower stance, more upright
Posterior chain: low-bar, wider stance, more hip hinge
Mobility limited: try heel-elevated or front squat first

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, squats are the most important leg lift you'll learn.
responses.explanation: Don't chase a number — chase clean reps. Bodyweight first until depth and bracing feel automatic, then add load slowly.
responses.suggestion: Start with goblet squats (dumbbell at chest) for 3 sets of 10.

patterns: how to set up for a deadlift
how to lock out a deadlift

actionSteps: Bar over mid-foot, shins ~1 inch from the bar
Hinge hips back, grip outside the knees
Chest up, lats tight (squeeze oranges in your armpits)
Push the floor away — don't yank the bar
Lock hips and knees out together, lower with control
responses.suggestion: Want a warm-up ramp for your working sets?

responses.shortAnswer: Deadlifts are a hip-hinge expression of full-body tension.
responses.explanation: RDLs train the eccentric hinge and bias hamstrings. Conventional pulls hit hamstrings + erectors. Sumo trades back stress for hip stress.
actionSteps: Conventional: shoulders slightly ahead of bar at start
Sumo: torso more upright, hips closer to bar
RDL: bar travels along legs, slight knee bend, stop at mid-shin

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, deadlifts look intimidating but the cue is simple: push the floor away.
responses.explanation: If your back rounds you're going too heavy. Drop weight, film a side angle, fix the hinge.
responses.suggestion: Try 3 sets of 5 with a weight you could do for 8 — leave reps in the tank.

patterns: how to do a bench press
what muscles does bench press work

actionSteps: Feet planted, slight upper-back arch, shoulder blades retracted
Grip just outside shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows
Lower bar to lower-mid chest, elbows ~45° from torso
Drive feet into the floor as you press the bar back up
Lockout straight up — don't drift toward your face
responses.suggestion: Want a spot or a safety-bar setup tip?

responses.shortAnswer: Bench press is chest, anterior delts, and triceps — biased by grip and angle.
responses.explanation: Close grip emphasizes triceps; wide grip biases pecs but stresses shoulders. Incline shifts work to upper chest and front delts.
actionSteps: Close grip: index fingers ~16in apart, elbows tight
Incline: 30° is the sweet spot for upper chest
Flared elbows = shoulder pain — keep them tucked

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, the bench press is a full-body lift — not just a chest exercise.
responses.explanation: Leg drive, retracted shoulder blades, and a tight upper back all add to the press. Treat it like a squat lying down.
responses.suggestion: Always bench with a spotter or in a power rack with safety pins.

patterns: how to do a pull up
how to get my first pullup
wide grip vs neutral grip pullup

actionSteps: Hang from the bar, hands just outside shoulder-width
Set your shoulders down — pull your shoulder blades into your back pockets
Drive elbows down and back, chest toward the bar
Chin clears the bar at the top, full hang at the bottom
Control the lower — eccentric is where the gains live
responses.suggestion: Want a no-pullup-yet progression plan?

responses.shortAnswer: Pull-ups train lats, biceps, mid-back, and grip.
responses.explanation: Chin-ups (underhand) are easier and more biceps-dominant. Wide grip biases lats more, neutral grip is shoulder-friendly.
actionSteps: Chin-up: supinated grip, shoulder-width — easier, more biceps
Pull-up: pronated, just outside shoulder-width — harder, more lats
Neutral grip: palms facing — best for cranky shoulders

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, can't do one yet? Build up — every strong puller started here.
responses.explanation: The fastest path is heavy negatives + assisted reps. Don't grind 100 bad-form reps — build the pattern correctly.
actionSteps: Negatives: jump to top, lower for 5 seconds. 3 sets of 5
Band-assisted: loop a band around the bar, foot in it
Inverted rows: feet on floor, bar at chest height — same pattern, easier

patterns: is cardio bad for muscle gain

responses.shortAnswer: Cardio: 2–4 sessions/week, 20–40 min each, mix intensities.
actionSteps: 2× zone 2 (conversational pace) for heart health and recovery
1–2× higher intensity (HIIT or tempo) for fitness
Schedule cardio AFTER lifting on the same day, or on rest days
Lower-impact options (bike, row, incline walk) save your joints
responses.suggestion: Want a sample weekly cardio template?

responses.shortAnswer: Steady-state and HIIT do different jobs — both belong in a program.
responses.explanation: Zone 2 builds aerobic capacity and recovery; HIIT raises VO2max but is very fatiguing. Excessive HIIT eats into lifting recovery — keep it 1–2× per week.
actionSteps: Zone 2: heart rate 60–70% of max, 30–60 min, can hold a conversation
If lifting and cardio same day, lift first

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, cardio doesn't have to be punishing.
responses.explanation: A brisk 30-min walk on incline 5× a week is more useful than smashing yourself once. Consistency beats intensity here.
responses.suggestion: Start with a 20-minute walk after dinner. That's it. You can build from there.

patterns: how many calories to lose weight

responses.shortAnswer: Fat loss in 4 levers — pick all four for fastest progress.
actionSteps: Eat in a 300–500 kcal deficit (not more — it backfires)
Hit 1.6–2.2g of protein per kg of bodyweight to keep muscle
Lift heavy 3–5×/week — gives your body a reason to keep muscle
Walk 8–10k steps/day; this is the cheapest cardio there is
responses.suggestion: Want help estimating your daily calories?

responses.shortAnswer: Slow cuts (0.5–1% bodyweight/week) preserve muscle and sanity.
responses.explanation: Aggressive cuts work for ~3 weeks then crash adherence. The deficit is the king variable; protein protects muscle; cardio is a tool, not the answer.
actionSteps: Track calories for 1 week to learn your maintenance
Subtract 300–500 kcal — that's your cutting target
Re-weigh weekly average; if not down in 2–3 weeks, cut another 10%

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, the boring answer is the only one that works: small deficit + high protein + lifting.
responses.explanation: There's no special food, no magic timing, no detox tea. Calories in vs calories out, with enough protein to keep muscle.
responses.suggestion: Pick ONE habit this week — track protein, hit 8k steps, or log every meal. Just one.

patterns: skinny guy how to gain muscle
how much to eat to gain muscle

responses.shortAnswer: Muscle growth = progressive overload + slight calorie surplus + protein.
actionSteps: Hit 1.6–2.2g of protein per kg of bodyweight
Train 4–6 sets per muscle group, 5–30 reps per set, 8–20 weekly sets
Progressive overload: add reps OR weight each week
responses.suggestion: Want a 4-day hypertrophy split?

responses.shortAnswer: Volume + proximity to failure are the two non-negotiables.
responses.explanation: Most lifters under-eat or under-train. 10–20 hard sets per muscle per week, taken close to failure (RIR 0–3), in a small surplus is the recipe.
actionSteps: Compounds for the bulk of stimulus, isolations for finishing
Track top set weight × reps weekly — must trend up
Sleep 7–9 hours; under-recovered tissue can't grow

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, you'll grow muscle if you eat enough, lift hard, and sleep.
responses.explanation: Newcomers grow fastest because everything's a stimulus. Don't overcomplicate — show up, hit your sets, get bigger.
responses.suggestion: Eat one extra meal a day, hit your protein, and add weight to the bar weekly.

responses.shortAnswer: Only 3 supplements have strong evidence — the rest are optional.
actionSteps: **Creatine monohydrate**: 5g/day, take whenever. Most-studied performance supp.
**Whey protein**: a convenient way to hit your daily protein target — not magic
**Vitamin D + omega-3**: useful for most people, especially in winter
Skip BCAAs if you eat enough protein. Skip fat burners — they don't work.
responses.suggestion: Want help calculating your daily protein target?

responses.shortAnswer: Creatine, whey, and caffeine are the only supps with consistent meta-analytic support.
responses.explanation: Creatine adds ~5–10% strength over months. Whey is just protein — same as eggs and chicken. BCAAs are redundant if total protein is met. Pre-workouts are mostly caffeine + flavor.
actionSteps: Creatine: 5g/day, no loading needed unless you're impatient
Whey: 25–40g per shake, 1–2 shakes/day if needed

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, supplements are the last 5% — diet and training are the first 95%.
responses.explanation: Don't waste money on stacks until you're consistently hitting protein, calories, sleep, and progressive overload.
responses.suggestion: If you take only one thing: 5g of creatine monohydrate a day.

responses.shortAnswer: {firstName}, {totalWorkouts} workouts in. That's a habit, not a phase.
responses.explanation: {streak} days strong right now (your best was {longestStreak}). Weight {weightDirectionWord} {weightDeltaKg}kg, plan adherence {adherenceLabel}. You're building something real.
